title: Quantum-classical solvation hydrodynamics: Hamiltonian functionals and dissipation
abstract: We propose a mixed quantum-classical hydrodynamic framework to model short-time inertial effects in the non-adiabatic evolution of a quantum solute coupled to a classical polar solvent. Drawing upon the work of Burghardt and Bagchi [Chem. Phys. 329 (2006), 343], we employ the Hamiltonian approach to incorporate consistent backreaction and preserve quantum decoherence beyond standard Ehrenfest dynamics. The solvent is treated as an ideal polar fluid and the quantum solute state is correlated to both the position and molecular orientation coordinates of the liquid. This approach retains essential solute-solvent correlations while significantly reducing the computational complexity of previous approaches. We further incorporate dissipative terms to capture both inertial effects and polarization relaxation. After establishing the general setting for non-local dielectric continua, the Marcus local approximation is integrated into the model thereby extending traditional solvation theory to account for collective fluid sloshing on fast timescales.
summary: This paper presents a new hydrodynamic theory for modeling how a quantum molecule interacts with a classical liquid solvent. It overcomes the limitations of standard models by including essential physics like backreaction and solvent inertia, providing a mathematically consistent way to study ultrafast chemical dynamics.
main_problem: Developing a consistent mixed quantum-classical hydrodynamic framework to model non-adiabatic solvation, specifically capturing short-time inertial effects, backreaction, and solvent-induced decoherence.
main_result: The derivation of a set of coupled hydrodynamic equations that preserve the underlying Hamiltonian and Poisson structures, extending the model from simple Ehrenfest dynamics to a complex Koopman-based framework including dissipation and polarization.
method: The authors use a moment method from kinetic theory applied to the Koopman model, employing a Hamiltonian functional approach and Lie-Poisson brackets to ensure energy balance and the preservation of Casimir invariants.
model_or_system: A hybrid system consisting of a quantum solute (e.g., NO molecule) interacting with a classical polar solvent (e.g., supercritical Argon), modeled using a Koopman-based hydrodynamic framework with a Marcus local approximation.
key_observables: Fluid density, momentum density, quantum density matrix, polarization, angular momentum, and entropy.
important_parameters: hbar (quantum scale), mass, microinertia constant, electric dipole moment, viscosity, and polarization diffusion coefficient.
main_assumptions: The solvent is treated as an ideal polar fluid; the model assumes the validity of the Koopman wavefunction approach and uses a cold-fluid ansatz to simplify the hydrodynamic closure.
paper_structure: The paper begins by establishing a Hamiltonian baseline for nonpolar solvation, progresses to a complex polar model with orientational backreaction, introduces the Koopman model to capture decoherence, and concludes by integrating dissipative terms for viscosity and polarization relaxation.
why_interesting: It provides a rigorous mathematical framework for studying open quantum systems coupled to classical environments, specifically addressing how backreaction and dissipation affect quantum decoherence and non-adiabatic dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
